metadata.title: Dostoevsky: Better Space-Time Trade-Offs for LSM-Tree Based Key-Value Stores via Adaptive Removal of Superfluous Merging
metadata.titleTranslation: Dostoevsky：通过自适应移除多余合并，为基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储实现更优的时空权衡
metadata.venue: Proceedings of the 2018 International Conference on Management of Data (SIGMOD ’18)
metadata.source: Dayan和Idreos - 2018 - Dostoevsky Better Space-Time Trade-Offs for LSM-Tree Based Key-Value Stores via Adaptive Removal of(3).pdf
metadata.notes: 正文覆盖 PDF 第 1–12 页；已排除作者单位、出版声明、Acknowledgments、References 及附录 A–I。

sections.title: Abstract
sections.originalMarkdown:
We show that all mainstream LSM-tree based key-value stores in the literature and in industry suboptimally trade between the I/O cost of updates on one hand and the I/O cost of lookups and storage space on the other. The reason is that they perform equally expensive merge operations across all levels of LSM-tree to bound the number of runs that a lookup has to probe and to remove obsolete entries to reclaim storage space. With state-of-the-art designs, however, merge operations from all levels of LSM-tree but the largest (i.e., most merge operations) reduce point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and storage space by a negligible amount while significantly adding to the amortized cost of updates.

To address this problem, we introduce Lazy Leveling, a new design that removes merge operations from all levels of LSM-tree but the largest. Lazy Leveling improves the worst-case complexity of update cost while maintaining the same bounds on point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and storage space. We further introduce Fluid LSM-tree, a generalization of the entire LSM-tree design space that can be parameterized to assume any existing design. Relative to Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-tree can optimize more for updates by merging less at the largest level, or it can optimize more for short range lookups by merging more at all other levels.

We put everything together to design Dostoevsky, a key-value store that adaptively removes superfluous merging by navigating the Fluid LSM-tree design space based on the application workload and hardware. We implemented Dostoevsky on top of RocksDB, and we show that it strictly dominates state-of-the-art designs in terms of performance and storage space.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们证明，文献界和工业界所有主流的基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储，在一方面的更新 I/O 成本与另一方面的查询 I/O 成本和存储空间之间所作的权衡均非最优。其原因在于，它们会在 LSM-tree 的所有层执行成本同等高昂的合并操作，以限制查询必须探查的有序游程数量，并移除过时条目以回收存储空间。然而，在最先进的设计中，除最大层以外的所有 LSM-tree 层上的合并操作（即绝大多数合并操作）仅能以可忽略的幅度降低点查询成本、长范围查询成本和存储空间，却会显著增加更新的摊销成本。

为了解决这一问题，我们提出 Lazy Leveling，这是一种新设计，它移除了 LSM-tree 中除最大层以外所有层上的合并操作。Lazy Leveling 在保持点查询成本、长范围查询成本和存储空间界限不变的同时，改善了更新成本的最坏情况复杂度。我们还提出 Fluid LSM-tree，它是对整个 LSM-tree 设计空间的泛化，可通过参数配置来呈现任何现有设计。相对于 Lazy Leveling，Fluid LSM-tree 可以通过减少最大层的合并来进一步针对更新优化，也可以通过增加其他所有层的合并来进一步针对短范围查询优化。

我们将上述思想整合起来，设计了 Dostoevsky：一种根据应用工作负载和硬件在 Fluid LSM-tree 设计空间中进行导航，从而自适应移除多余合并的键值存储。我们在 RocksDB 之上实现了 Dostoevsky，并证明它在性能和存储空间方面严格支配最先进的设计。

sections.title: 1 INTRODUCTION
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Key-Value Stores and LSM-Trees.** A key-value store is a database that efficiently maps from search keys to their corresponding data values. Key-value stores are used everywhere today from graph processing in social media [8, 17] to event log processing in cyber security [18] to online transaction processing [27]. To persist key-value entries in storage, most key-value stores today use LSM-tree [41]. LSM-tree buffers inserted/updated entries in main memory and flushes the buffer as a sorted run to secondary storage every time that it fills up. LSM-tree later sort-merges these runs to bound the number of runs that a lookup has to probe and to remove obsolete entries, i.e., for which there exists a more recent entry with the same key. LSM-tree organizes runs into levels of exponentially increasing capacities whereby larger levels contain older runs. As entries are updated out-of-place, a point lookup finds the most recent version of an entry by probing the levels from smallest to largest and terminating when it finds the target key. A range lookup, on the other hand, has to access the relevant key range from across all runs at all levels and to eliminate obsolete entries from the result set. To speed up lookups on individual runs, modern designs maintain two additional structures in main memory. First, for every run there is a set of fence pointers that contain the first key of every block of the run; this allows lookups to access a particular key within a run with just one I/O. Second, for every run there exists a Bloom filter; this allows point lookups to skip runs that do not contain the target key. This overall design is adopted in a large number of modern key-value stores including LevelDB [32] and BigTable [19] at Google, RocksDB [29] at Facebook, Cassandra [34], HBase [7] and Accumulo [5] at Apache, Voldemort [38] at LinkedIn, Dynamo [26] at Amazon, WiredTiger [52] at MongoDB, and bLSM [48] and cLSM [31] at Yahoo. Relational databases today such as MySQL (using MyRocks [28]) and SQLite4 support this design too as a storage engine by mapping primary keys to rows as values.

*Figure 1: Dostoevsky enables richer space-time trade-offs among updates, point lookups, range lookups and space-amplification, and it navigates the design space to find the best trade-off for a particular application.*

**The Problem.** The frequency of merge operations in LSM-tree controls an intrinsic trade-off between the I/O cost of updates on one hand and the I/O cost of lookups and storage space-amplification (i.e., caused by the presence of obsolete entries) on the other. The problem is that existing designs trade suboptimally among these metrics. Figure 1 conceptually depicts this by plotting point lookup cost and space-amplification on the y-axis against update cost on the x-axis (while these y-axis metrics have different units, their trade-off curves with respect to the x-axis have the same shape). The two points at the edges of the curves are a log and a sorted array. LSM-tree degenerates into these edge points when it does not merge at all or when it merges as much as possible, respectively. We place mainstream systems along the top curve between these edge points based on their default merge frequencies, and we draw a superior trade-off curve for Monkey [22], which represents the current state of the art. We show that there exists an even superior trade-off curve to Monkey. Existing designs forgo a significant amount of performance and/or storage space for not being designed along this bottom curve.

**The Problem’s Source.** By analyzing the design space of state-of-the-art LSM-trees, we pinpoint the problem to the fact that the worst-case update cost, point lookup cost, range lookup cost, and space-amplification derive differently from across different levels.

- **Updates.** The I/O cost of an update is paid later through the merge operations that the updated entry participates in. While merge operations at larger levels entail exponentially more work, they take place exponentially less frequently. Therefore, updates derive their I/O cost equally from merge operations across all levels.
- **Point lookups.** While mainstream designs along the top curve in Figure 1 set the same false positive rate to Bloom filters across all levels of LSM-tree, Monkey, the current state of the art, sets exponentially lower false positive rates to Bloom filters at smaller levels [22]. This is shown to minimize the sum of false positive rates across all filters and to thereby minimize I/O for point lookups. At the same time, this means that access to smaller levels is exponentially less probable. Therefore, most point lookup I/Os target the largest level.
- **Long range lookups¹.** As levels in LSM-tree have exponentially increasing capacities, the largest level contains most of the data, and so it tends to contain most of the entries within a given key-range. Therefore, most I/Os issued by long range lookups target the largest level.
- **Short range lookups.** Range lookups with extremely small key ranges only access approximately one block within each run regardless of the run’s size. As the maximum number of runs per level is fixed in state-of-the-art designs, short range lookups derive their I/O cost equally from across all levels.
- **Space-Amplification.** The worst-case space-amplification occurs when all entries at smaller levels are updates to entries at the largest level. Therefore, the highest fraction of obsolete entries in the worst-case is at the largest level.

Since the worst-case point lookup cost, long range lookup cost and space-amplification derive mostly from the largest level, merge operations at all levels of LSM-tree but the largest (i.e., most merge operations) hardly improve on these metrics while significantly adding to the amortized cost of updates. This leads to suboptimal trade-offs. We solve this problem from the ground up in three steps.

¹ In Section 3, we distinguish formally between short and long range lookups.

**Solution 1: Lazy Leveling to Remove Superfluous Merging.** We expand the LSM-tree design space with Lazy Leveling, a new design that removes merging from all but the largest level of LSM-tree. Lazy Leveling improves the worst-case cost complexity of updates while maintaining the same bounds on point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and space-amplification and while providing a competitive bound on short range lookup cost. We show that the improved update cost can be traded to reduce point lookup cost and space-amplification. This generates the bottom curve in Figure 1, which offers richer space-time trade-offs that have been impossible to achieve with state-of-the-art designs until now.

**Solution 2: Fluid LSM-Tree for Design Space Fluidity.** We introduce Fluid LSM-tree as a generalization of LSM-tree that enables transitioning fluidly across the whole LSM-tree design space. Fluid LSM-tree does this by controlling the frequency of merge operations separately for the largest level and for all other levels. Relative to Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-tree can optimize more for updates by merging less at the largest level, or it can optimize more for short range lookups by merging more at all other levels.

**Solution 3: Dostoevsky to Navigate the Design Space.** We put everything together in Dostoevsky: Space-Time Optimized Evolvable Scalable Key-Value Store. Dostoevsky analytically finds the tuning of Fluid LSM-tree that maximizes throughput for a particular application workload and hardware subject to a user constraint on space-amplification. It does this by pruning the search space to quickly find the best tuning and physically adapting to it during runtime. Since Dostoevsky spans all existing designs and is able to navigate to the best one for a given application, it strictly dominates existing key-value stores in terms of performance and space-amplification. We depict Dostoevsky in Figure 1 as a black star that can navigate the entire design space.

**Contributions.** Our contributions are summarized below.

- We show that state-of-the-art LSM-trees perform equally expensive merge operations across all levels of LSM-tree, yet merge operations at all but the largest level (i.e., most merge operations) improve point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and space-amplification by a negligible amount while adding significantly to the amortized cost of updates.
- We introduce Lazy Leveling to remove merge operations at all but the largest level. This improves the cost complexity of updates while maintaining the same bounds on point lookups, long range lookups, and space-amplification and while providing a competitive bound on short range lookups.
- We introduce Fluid LSM-tree, a generalization of LSM-tree that spans all existing designs. Relative to Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-tree can optimize more for updates by merging less at the largest level, or it can optimize more for short range lookups by merging more at all other levels.
- We introduce Dostoevsky, a key-value store that dynamically adapts across the Fluid LSM-tree design space to the design that maximizes the worst-case throughput based on the application workload and the hardware subject to a constraint on space-amplification.
- We implemented Dostoevsky on RocksDB and show that it dominates existing designs for any application scenario.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**键值存储与 LSM-tree。** 键值存储是一种将查找键高效映射到对应数据值的数据库。如今，键值存储应用广泛，从社交媒体中的图处理 [8, 17]，到网络安全中的事件日志处理 [18]，再到在线事务处理 [27]。为了将键值条目持久化到存储设备，目前大多数键值存储都使用 LSM-tree [41]。LSM-tree 在主存中缓冲插入或更新的条目，并在缓冲区每次写满时，将其作为一个排序后的有序游程刷写到二级存储。随后，LSM-tree 对这些有序游程执行排序合并，以限制查询必须探查的有序游程数量，并移除过时条目，即存在具有相同键但版本更新的条目。LSM-tree 将有序游程组织成容量指数增长的多层，其中较大的层包含较旧的有序游程。由于条目采用异地更新，点查询会从最小层到最大层依次探查，并在找到目标键时终止，以此找到条目的最新版本。另一方面，范围查询必须访问所有层中所有有序游程上的相关键范围，并从结果集中消除过时条目。为了加速对单个有序游程的查询，现代设计在主存中维护两种附加结构。首先，每个有序游程都有一组围栏指针，其中包含该有序游程每个块的第一个键；借助它们，查询只需一次 I/O 就能访问有序游程中的特定键。其次，每个有序游程都有一个布隆过滤器；它使点查询能够跳过不含目标键的有序游程。大量现代键值存储都采用了这一整体设计，包括 Google 的 LevelDB [32] 和 BigTable [19]、Facebook 的 RocksDB [29]、Apache 的 Cassandra [34]、HBase [7] 和 Accumulo [5]、LinkedIn 的 Voldemort [38]、Amazon 的 Dynamo [26]、MongoDB 的 WiredTiger [52]，以及 Yahoo 的 bLSM [48] 和 cLSM [31]。如今，MySQL（使用 MyRocks [28]）和 SQLite4 等关系数据库也支持将这一设计用作存储引擎，即把主键映射到作为值的行。

*图 1：Dostoevsky 在更新、点查询、范围查询和空间放大之间实现了更丰富的时空权衡，并在设计空间中进行导航，以找到适合特定应用的最佳权衡。*

**问题。** LSM-tree 中合并操作的频率控制着一种内在权衡：一方面是更新的 I/O 成本，另一方面是查询的 I/O 成本和存储空间放大（即由过时条目的存在所导致）。问题在于，现有设计在这些指标之间作出的权衡并非最优。图 1 从概念上描绘了这一点：纵轴为点查询成本和空间放大，横轴为更新成本（尽管纵轴上的这些指标单位不同，但它们相对于横轴的权衡曲线具有相同形状）。曲线两端的两个点分别是日志和排序数组。当 LSM-tree 完全不合并或尽可能多地合并时，它会分别退化为这两个端点。我们根据主流系统的默认合并频率，将它们置于两个端点之间的上方曲线上；同时为代表当前最先进水平的 Monkey [22] 绘制了一条更优的权衡曲线。我们证明，还存在一条比 Monkey 更优的权衡曲线。由于未沿这条底部曲线进行设计，现有设计损失了大量性能和/或存储空间。

**问题的根源。** 通过分析最先进 LSM-tree 的设计空间，我们将问题定位到这样一个事实：最坏情况下的更新成本、点查询成本、范围查询成本和空间放大来自不同层的方式各不相同。

- **更新。** 一次更新的 I/O 成本会在之后通过该更新条目参与的合并操作来支付。尽管较大层上的合并操作涉及指数级更多的工作，但它们发生的频率也呈指数级降低。因此，更新的 I/O 成本等量来源于所有层上的合并操作。
- **点查询。** 图 1 上方曲线中的主流设计为 LSM-tree 所有层的布隆过滤器设置相同的假阳性率，而当前最先进的 Monkey 则为较小层的布隆过滤器设置指数级更低的假阳性率 [22]。已有研究证明，这能使所有过滤器的假阳性率之和最小，从而使点查询的 I/O 最少。与此同时，这也意味着访问较小层的概率呈指数级降低。因此，大多数点查询 I/O 都针对最大层。
- **长范围查询¹。** 由于 LSM-tree 各层容量呈指数增长，最大层包含绝大部分数据，因此它往往也包含给定键范围内的大多数条目。所以，长范围查询发出的大多数 I/O 都针对最大层。
- **短范围查询。** 键范围极小的范围查询，无论有序游程大小如何，在每个有序游程内都只访问大约一个块。由于最先进设计中每层的最大有序游程数量是固定的，因此短范围查询的 I/O 成本等量来源于所有层。
- **空间放大。** 最坏情况下的空间放大发生在较小层中的所有条目都是对最大层条目的更新时。因此，在最坏情况下，最大层中的过时条目占比最高。

由于最坏情况下的点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大主要来自最大层，LSM-tree 中除最大层以外所有层上的合并操作（即绝大多数合并操作）对这些指标几乎没有改善，却会显著增加更新的摊销成本。这导致了非最优的权衡。我们分三步从根本上解决这一问题。

¹ 在第 3 节中，我们会形式化地区分短范围查询和长范围查询。

**解决方案 1：用 Lazy Leveling 移除多余合并。** 我们以 Lazy Leveling 扩展 LSM-tree 的设计空间。这是一种新设计，它移除了 LSM-tree 中除最大层以外的所有合并。Lazy Leveling 在保持点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大的界限不变，并为短范围查询提供有竞争力界限的同时，改善了更新的最坏情况成本复杂度。我们证明，可以用改善后的更新成本来换取更低的点查询成本和空间放大。这形成了图 1 中的底部曲线，提供了在此之前最先进设计无法实现的、更丰富的时空权衡。

**解决方案 2：以 Fluid LSM-Tree 实现设计空间的流动性。** 我们提出 Fluid LSM-tree，将其作为 LSM-tree 的一种泛化，使系统能够在整个 LSM-tree 设计空间中平滑过渡。Fluid LSM-tree 通过分别控制最大层与其他所有层的合并操作频率来实现这一点。相对于 Lazy Leveling，Fluid LSM-tree 可以通过减少最大层上的合并来进一步针对更新优化，也可以通过增加其他所有层上的合并来进一步针对短范围查询优化。

**解决方案 3：用 Dostoevsky 在设计空间中导航。** 我们将所有思想整合到 Dostoevsky 中，其全称为时空优化、可演进、可扩展键值存储（Space-Time Optimized Evolvable Scalable Key-Value Store）。在用户给定空间放大约束的条件下，Dostoevsky 通过分析为特定应用工作负载和硬件找到能够最大化吞吐量的 Fluid LSM-tree 参数配置。它通过剪枝搜索空间快速找到最佳配置，并在运行期间通过物理调整适应该配置。由于 Dostoevsky 覆盖所有现有设计，并能够针对给定应用导航到最佳设计，因此它在性能和空间放大方面严格支配现有键值存储。我们在图 1 中将 Dostoevsky 描绘为一颗可以在整个设计空间中移动的黑色星标。

**贡献。** 我们的贡献概括如下。

- 我们证明，最先进的 LSM-tree 会在所有层上执行成本同等高昂的合并操作；然而，除最大层以外所有层上的合并操作（即绝大多数合并操作）对点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大的改善微乎其微，却会显著增加更新的摊销成本。
- 我们提出 Lazy Leveling，以移除除最大层以外所有层上的合并操作。这在保持点查询、长范围查询和空间放大的界限不变，并为短范围查询提供有竞争力界限的同时，改善了更新的成本复杂度。
- 我们提出 Fluid LSM-tree，它是对 LSM-tree 的泛化，覆盖所有现有设计。相对于 Lazy Leveling，Fluid LSM-tree 可以通过减少最大层上的合并来进一步针对更新优化，也可以通过增加其他所有层上的合并来进一步针对短范围查询优化。
- 我们提出 Dostoevsky，这是一种能够在 Fluid LSM-tree 设计空间中动态适应的键值存储；在空间放大约束下，它会根据应用工作负载和硬件适应到使最坏情况吞吐量最大化的设计。
- 我们在 RocksDB 上实现了 Dostoevsky，并证明它在任何应用场景下都支配现有设计。

sections.title: 2 BACKGROUND
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section gives the necessary background on LSM-tree based key-value stores. Figure 2 illustrates their architecture and Table 1 gives a list of terms we use throughout the paper.

*Figure 2: An overview of an LSM-tree using the tiering and leveling merge policies.*

**Table 1: Table of terms used throughput the paper.**

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | total number of entries | entries |
| $L$ | number of levels | levels |
| $L_{max}$ | maximum possible number of levels | levels |
| $B$ | number of entries that fit into a storage block | entries |
| $P$ | size of the buffer in storage blocks | blocks |
| $T$ | size ratio between adjacent levels | |
| $T_{lim}$ | size ratio at which $L$ converges to 1 | |
| $M$ | main memory allocated to the Bloom filters | bits |
| $p_i$ | false positive rate for Bloom filters at Level $i$ | |
| $s$ | selectivity of a range lookup | entries |
| $R$ | zero-result point lookup cost | I/Os |
| $V$ | non-zero-result point lookup cost | I/Os |
| $Q$ | range lookup cost | I/Os |
| $W$ | update cost | I/Os |
| $K$ | bound on number of runs at Levels 1 to $L - 1$ | runs |
| $Z$ | bound on number of runs at Level $L$ | runs |
| $\mu$ | storage sequential over random access speed | |
| $\phi$ | storage write over read speed | |

**LSM-Tree Structure.** LSM-tree optimizes for write-heavy workloads. This is an important performance goal for systems today because the proportion of application writes is continuously increasing (e.g., in 2012 Yahoo! reported that the proportion of writes targeting their web-service was 50% and projected to continue accelerating [48]). To optimize for writes, LSM-tree initially buffers all updates, insertions, and deletes (henceforth referred to as updates unless otherwise mentioned) in main memory, as shown in Figure 2. When the buffer fills up, LSM-tree flushes the buffer to secondary storage as a sorted run. LSM-tree sort-merges runs in order to (1) bound the number of runs that a lookup has to access in secondary storage, and to (2) remove obsolete entries to reclaim space. It organizes runs into $L$ conceptual levels of exponentially increasing sizes. Level 0 is the buffer in main memory, and runs belonging to all other levels are in secondary storage.

The balance between the I/O cost of merging and the I/O cost of lookups and space-amplification can be tuned using two knobs. The first knob is the size ratio $T$ between the capacities of adjacent levels; $T$ controls the number of levels of LSM-tree and thus the overall number of times that an entry gets merged across levels. The second knob is the merge policy, which controls the number of times an entry gets merged within a level. All designs today use either one of two merge policies: tiering or leveling (e.g., Cassandra and RocksDB use tiering and leveling by default, respectively [6, 29]). With tiering, we merge runs within a level only when the level reaches capacity [33]. With leveling, we merge runs within a level whenever a new run comes in [41]. We compare these policies in Figure 2 (with a size ratio of 4 and a buffer size of one entry²). In both cases, the merge is triggered by the buffer flushing and causing Level 1 to reach capacity. With tiering, all runs at Level 1 get merged into a new run that gets placed at Level 2. With leveling, the merge also includes the preexisting run at Level 2. We formally study this design space in the next section. We discuss further details of merge mechanics in Appendix D and other log-structured designs in Appendix E.

² In practice, the buffer size is typically set between 2 and 64 MB [29, 32], but we use a size of one entry in this example for ease of illustration.

**Number of Levels.** The buffer at Level 0 has a capacity of $B \cdot P$ entries, where $B$ is the number of entries that fit into a storage block, and $P$ is the size of the buffer in terms of storage blocks. In general, Level $i$ has a capacity of $B \cdot P \cdot T^i$ entries, and the capacity at the largest level can be approximated as having $N \cdot \frac{T-1}{T}$ entries. To derive the number of levels, we divide the capacity at the largest level by the capacity of the buffer and take log base $T$ of the quotient, as shown in Equation 1.

$$L = \left\lceil \log_T \left(\frac{N}{B \cdot P} \cdot \frac{T-1}{T}\right) \right\rceil$$

We restrict the size ratio to the domain of $2 \le T \le T_{lim}$, where $T_{lim}$ is defined as $\frac{N}{B \cdot P}$. As the size ratio increases and approaches $T_{lim}$, the number of levels decreases and approaches 1. Increasing $T$ beyond $T_{lim}$ has no structural impact. Furthermore, restricting $T$ to be 2 or greater ensures that the resulting run from a merge operation at level $i$ is never large enough to move beyond level $i + 1$. In other words, this ensures that runs do not skip levels. Thus, the highest possible number of levels $L_{max}$ is $\left\lceil \log_2 \left(\frac{N}{B \cdot P} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\right) \right\rceil$ (i.e., when the size ratio is set to 2).

**Finding Entries.** Since entries are updated out-of-place, multiple versions of an entry with the same key may exist across multiple levels (and even across runs within a level with tiering). To ensure that a lookup is always able to find the most recent version of an entry, LSM-tree takes the following measures. (1) When an entry is inserted into the buffer and the buffer already contains an entry with the same key, the newer entry replaces the older one. (2) When two runs that contain an entry with the same key are merged, only the entry from the newer run is kept because it is more recent. (3) To be able to infer the order at which different entries with the same key across different runs were inserted, a run can only be merged with the next older or the next younger run. Overall, these rules ensure that if there are two runs that contain different versions of the same entry, the younger run contains the newer version.

**Point Lookups.** A point lookup finds the most recent version of an entry by traversing the levels from smallest to largest, and runs within a level from youngest to oldest with tiering. It terminates when it finds the first entry with a matching key.

**Range Lookups.** A range lookup has to find the most recent versions of all entries within the target key range. It does this by sort-merging the relevant key range across all runs at all levels. While sort-merging, it identifies entries with the same key across different runs and discards older versions.

**Deletes.** Deletes are supported by adding a one-bit flag to every entry. If a lookup finds that the most recent version of an entry has this flag on, it does not return a value to the application. When a deleted entry is merged with the oldest run, it is discarded as it has replaced all entries with the same key that were inserted prior to it.

**Fragmented Merging.** To smooth out performance slumps due to long merge operations at larger levels, mainstream designs partition runs into files (e.g., 2 to 64 MB [29, 32]) called Sorted String Tables (SSTables), and they merge one SSTable at a time with SSTables with an overlapping key range at the next older run. This technique does not affect the worst-case I/O overhead of merging but only how this overhead gets scheduled across time. For readability throughout the paper, we discuss merge operations as having the granularity of runs, though they can also have the granularity of SSTables.

**Space-Amplification.** The factor by which the presence of obsolete entries amplify storage space is known as space-amplification. Space-amplification has traditionally not been a major concern for data structure design due to the affordability of disks. The advent of SSDs, however, makes space-amplification an important cost concern (e.g., Facebook has recently switched from B-trees to leveled LSM-trees due to their superior space-amplification properties [27]). We include space-amplification as a cost metric to give a complete picture of the designs that we introduce and evaluate.

**Fence Pointers.** All major LSM-tree based key-value stores index the first key of every block of every run in main memory. We call these fence pointers (see Figure 2). Formally, the fence pointers take up $O(N/B)$ space in main memory, and they enable a lookup to find the relevant key-range at every run with one I/O.

**Bloom Filters.** To speed up point lookups, which are common in practice [16, 48], each run has a Bloom filter [14] in main memory, as shown in Figure 2. A Bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure used to answer set membership queries. It cannot return a false negative, though it returns a false positive with a tunable false positive rate (FPR). The FPR depends on the ratio between the number of bits allocated to the filter and the number of entries in the set according to the following expression³ [50]:

$$FPR = e^{-(\mathrm{bits}/\mathrm{entries}) \cdot \ln(2)^2}$$

A point lookup probes a Bloom filter before accessing the corresponding run in storage. If the filter returns a true positive, the lookup accesses the run with one I/O (i.e., using the fence pointers), finds the matching entry, and terminates. If the filter returns a negative, the lookup skips the run thereby saving one I/O. Otherwise, we have a false positive, meaning the lookup wastes one I/O by accessing the run, not finding a matching entry, and having to continue searching for the target key in the next run.

Bloom filter has a useful property that if it is partitioned into smaller equally-sized Bloom filters with an equal division of entries among them, the FPR of each one of the new partitioned Bloom filters is asymptotically the same as the FPR of the original filter (though slightly higher in practice) [50]. For ease of discussion, we refer to Bloom filters as being non-partitioned, though they can also be partitioned (e.g., per every block of every run) as some designs in industry to enable greater flexibility for space management (e.g., Bloom filters for blocks that are not frequently read by point lookups can be offloaded to storage to save memory) [32].

³ Equation 2 assumes that the Bloom filter is using the optimal number of hash functions $\frac{\mathrm{bits}}{\mathrm{entries}} \cdot \ln(2)$ to minimize the false positive rate.

**Applicability Beyond Key-Value Stores.** In accordance with designs in industry, our discussion assumes that a key is stored adjacently to its value within a run [29, 32]. For readability, all figures in this paper depict entries as keys, but they represent key-value pairs. Our work also applies to applications where there are no values (i.e., the LSM-tree is used to answer set-membership queries on keys), where the values are pointers to data objects stored outside of LSM-tree [39], or where LSM-tree is used as a building block for solving a more complex algorithmic problem (e.g., graph analytics [17], flash translation layer design [23], etc.). We restrict the scope of analysis to the basic operations and size of LSM-tree so that it can easily be applied to each of these other cases.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节介绍基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储所需的背景知识。图 2 展示了其体系结构，表 1 列出了本文通篇使用的术语。

*图 2：采用分层式（tiering）和分级式（leveling）合并策略的 LSM-tree 概览。*

**表 1：本文通篇使用的术语表。**

| 术语 | 定义 | 单位 |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | 条目总数 | 条目 |
| $L$ | 层数 | 层 |
| $L_{max}$ | 可能的最大层数 | 层 |
| $B$ | 一个存储块可容纳的条目数 | 条目 |
| $P$ | 以存储块计的缓冲区大小 | 块 |
| $T$ | 相邻层之间的大小比率 | |
| $T_{lim}$ | 使 $L$ 收敛到 1 的大小比率 | |
| $M$ | 分配给布隆过滤器的主存 | 位 |
| $p_i$ | 第 $i$ 层布隆过滤器的假阳性率 | |
| $s$ | 范围查询的选择率 | 条目 |
| $R$ | 零结果点查询成本 | I/O |
| $V$ | 非零结果点查询成本 | I/O |
| $Q$ | 范围查询成本 | I/O |
| $W$ | 更新成本 | I/O |
| $K$ | 第 1 层至第 $L - 1$ 层有序游程数量的上界 | 有序游程 |
| $Z$ | 第 $L$ 层有序游程数量的上界 | 有序游程 |
| $\mu$ | 存储设备顺序访问速度与随机访问速度之比 | |
| $\phi$ | 存储设备写速度与读速度之比 | |

**LSM-tree 结构。** LSM-tree 针对写密集型工作负载进行了优化。这是当今系统的一项重要性能目标，因为应用写入的比例正在持续上升（例如，Yahoo! 在 2012 年报告称，针对其 Web 服务的写入比例为 50%，并预计这一比例会继续加速增长 [48]）。为了优化写入，LSM-tree 首先在主存中缓冲所有更新、插入和删除（除非另有说明，下文统称为更新），如图 2 所示。当缓冲区写满时，LSM-tree 将缓冲区作为一个排序后的有序游程刷写到二级存储。LSM-tree 对有序游程执行排序合并，目的是：(1) 限制查询必须在二级存储中访问的有序游程数量；(2) 移除过时条目以回收空间。它将有序游程组织成 $L$ 个容量呈指数增长的逻辑层。第 0 层是主存中的缓冲区，其他所有层的有序游程均位于二级存储中。

合并 I/O 成本与查询 I/O 成本及空间放大之间的平衡可以通过两个调节旋钮来调整。第一个旋钮是相邻层容量之间的大小比率 $T$；$T$ 控制 LSM-tree 的层数，进而控制条目跨层合并的总次数。第二个旋钮是合并策略，它控制条目在一层内部被合并的次数。如今所有设计都采用两种合并策略之一：分层式（tiering）或分级式（leveling）（例如，Cassandra 和 RocksDB 默认分别使用分层式与分级式策略 [6, 29]）。采用分层式策略时，只有当一层达到其容量上限时，我们才合并该层内的有序游程 [33]。采用分级式策略时，每当一个新的有序游程到达某层，我们都会合并该层内的有序游程 [41]。图 2 对比了这两种策略（大小比率为 4，缓冲区大小为一个条目²）。在两种情况下，缓冲区刷写都会导致第 1 层达到容量上限，从而触发合并。采用分层式策略时，第 1 层的所有有序游程被合并为一个新的有序游程，并放置到第 2 层。采用分级式策略时，合并还会包含第 2 层原有的有序游程。下一节将形式化研究这一设计空间。附录 D 讨论合并机制的更多细节，附录 E 讨论其他日志结构设计。

² 在实践中，缓冲区大小通常设为 2 至 64 MB [29, 32]，但为了便于说明，本例使用一个条目的大小。

**层数。** 第 0 层缓冲区的容量为 $B \cdot P$ 个条目，其中 $B$ 是一个存储块可容纳的条目数，$P$ 是以存储块计的缓冲区大小。一般而言，第 $i$ 层的容量为 $B \cdot P \cdot T^i$ 个条目，而最大层的容量可以近似为 $N \cdot \frac{T-1}{T}$ 个条目。为了推导层数，我们用最大层容量除以缓冲区容量，并对该商取以 $T$ 为底的对数，如公式 1 所示。

$$L = \left\lceil \log_T \left(\frac{N}{B \cdot P} \cdot \frac{T-1}{T}\right) \right\rceil$$

我们将大小比率限制在 $2 \le T \le T_{lim}$ 的范围内，其中 $T_{lim}$ 定义为 $\frac{N}{B \cdot P}$。随着大小比率增大并接近 $T_{lim}$，层数会减少并趋近于 1。继续将 $T$ 增大到 $T_{lim}$ 以上不会产生结构上的影响。此外，将 $T$ 限制为不小于 2，可以确保第 $i$ 层一次合并操作产生的有序游程绝不会大到需要越过第 $i + 1$ 层。换言之，这保证了有序游程不会跳过层。因此，可能的最大层数 $L_{max}$ 为 $\left\lceil \log_2 \left(\frac{N}{B \cdot P} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\right) \right\rceil$（即大小比率设为 2 时）。

**查找条目。** 由于条目采用异地更新，同一个键的多个条目版本可能存在于多个层中（采用分层式策略时，甚至可能存在于同一层内的多个有序游程中）。为确保查询始终能够找到条目的最新版本，LSM-tree 采取以下措施。(1) 当一个条目被插入缓冲区，而缓冲区中已经存在具有相同键的条目时，新条目替换旧条目。(2) 当两个包含相同键条目的有序游程被合并时，只保留较新的有序游程中的条目，因为它的版本更新。(3) 为了能够推断不同有序游程中相同键条目的插入顺序，一个有序游程只能与紧邻的更旧或更新的有序游程合并。总体而言，这些规则确保：如果两个有序游程包含同一条目的不同版本，则较新的有序游程中包含更新的版本。

**点查询。** 点查询从最小层到最大层遍历各层；采用分层式策略时，还会在一层内从最新到最旧遍历各有序游程，从而查找条目的最新版本。当找到第一个键匹配的条目时，查询终止。

**范围查询。** 范围查询必须找到目标键范围内所有条目的最新版本。为此，它会对所有层中所有有序游程上的相关键范围执行排序合并。在排序合并过程中，它识别不同有序游程中键相同的条目，并丢弃较旧版本。

**删除。** 系统通过为每个条目增加一个一位标志来支持删除。如果查询发现条目的最新版本设置了该标志，就不会向应用返回值。当被删除的条目与最旧的有序游程合并时，该条目会被丢弃，因为它已经替代了此前插入的、具有相同键的所有条目。

**碎片化合并。** 为了缓解较大层上的长时间合并操作导致的性能骤降，主流设计将有序游程划分为文件（例如 2 至 64 MB [29, 32]），这些文件称为排序字符串表（Sorted String Table，SSTable）；每次只合并一个 SSTable，并将其与下一个更旧有序游程中键范围重叠的 SSTable 合并。这项技术不会影响合并在最坏情况下的 I/O 开销，只会改变该开销在时间上的调度方式。为了保证全文可读性，我们以有序游程作为讨论合并操作的粒度，尽管合并也可以采用 SSTable 粒度。

**空间放大。** 过时条目的存在使存储空间增大的倍数称为空间放大。由于磁盘价格低廉，空间放大传统上并不是数据结构设计的主要关注点。然而，SSD 的出现使空间放大成为一个重要的成本问题（例如，由于分级式 LSM-tree 具有更优的空间放大性质，Facebook 最近已从 B-tree 转向分级式 LSM-tree [27]）。我们将空间放大纳入成本指标，以完整呈现本文提出并评估的各种设计。

**围栏指针。** 所有主流的基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储都会在主存中为每个有序游程的每个块索引其第一个键。我们将这些索引称为围栏指针（见图 2）。形式化地说，围栏指针占用主存中的 $O(N/B)$ 空间，并使查询只需一次 I/O 就能在每个有序游程中找到相关键范围。

**布隆过滤器。** 为了加速实践中常见的点查询 [16, 48]，如图 2 所示，每个有序游程在主存中都有一个布隆过滤器 [14]。布隆过滤器是一种空间高效的概率型数据结构，用于回答集合成员查询。它不会产生假阴性，但会以可调节的假阳性率（FPR）产生假阳性。FPR 取决于分配给过滤器的位数与集合中条目数之比，具体关系如以下表达式³所示 [50]：

$$FPR = e^{-(\mathrm{bits}/\mathrm{entries}) \cdot \ln(2)^2}$$

点查询在访问存储中对应的有序游程之前，会先探查布隆过滤器。如果过滤器返回真阳性，查询就会用一次 I/O 访问该有序游程（即使用围栏指针），找到匹配条目并终止。如果过滤器返回阴性，查询会跳过该有序游程，从而节省一次 I/O。否则，就发生了假阳性：查询访问该有序游程却没有找到匹配条目，因而浪费一次 I/O，并不得不继续在下一个有序游程中查找目标键。

布隆过滤器具有一个有用的性质：如果将其划分为多个大小相等的小布隆过滤器，并在它们之间平均分配条目，则每个新划分的布隆过滤器的 FPR 在渐近意义上与原过滤器相同（尽管在实践中会略高）[50]。为了便于讨论，我们将布隆过滤器视为未分区的；不过，也可以像工业界的一些设计那样对其分区（例如，为每个有序游程的每个块设置一个分区），以实现更灵活的空间管理（例如，可以将点查询不经常读取的块所对应的布隆过滤器卸载到存储设备，以节省内存）[32]。

³ 公式 2 假设布隆过滤器采用最优哈希函数数量 $\frac{\mathrm{bits}}{\mathrm{entries}} \cdot \ln(2)$，以最小化假阳性率。

**键值存储之外的适用性。** 与工业界的设计一致，我们的讨论假设在一个有序游程内，键与其值相邻存储 [29, 32]。为便于阅读，本文所有图都将条目描绘为键，但它们实际代表键值对。我们的工作也适用于以下应用：不存在值的应用（即使用 LSM-tree 回答关于键的集合成员查询）；值是指向 LSM-tree 外部所存数据对象的指针的应用 [39]；或者将 LSM-tree 用作解决更复杂算法问题的构件的应用（例如图分析 [17]、闪存转换层设计 [23] 等）。我们将分析范围限制在 LSM-tree 的基本操作和大小上，使分析能够方便地应用于上述每一种情形。

sections.title: 3 DESIGN SPACE AND PROBLEM ANALYSIS
sections.originalMarkdown:
We now analyze how the worse-case space-amplification and I/O costs of updates and lookups derive from across different levels with respect to the merge policy and size ratio. To analyze updates and lookups, we use the disk access model [1] to count the number of I/Os per operation, where an I/O is a block read or written from secondary storage. The results are summarized in Figures 3 and 4.

**Analyzing Updates.** The I/O cost of updating an entry is paid through the subsequent merge operations that the updated entry participates in. Our analysis assumes a worst-case workload whereby all updates target entries at the largest level. This means that an obsolete entry does not get removed until its corresponding updated entry has reached the largest level. As a result, every entry gets merged across all levels (i.e., rather than getting discarded at some smaller level by a more recent entry and thereby reducing overhead for later merge operations).

With tiering, an entry gets merged $O(1)$ time per level across $O(L)$ levels. We divide this by the block size $B$ since every I/O during a merge operation copies $B$ entries from the original runs to the new run. Thus, the amortized I/O cost for one update is $O(\frac{L}{B})$ I/O. With leveling, the $j$th run that arrives at Level $i$ triggers a merge operation involving the existing run at Level $i$, which is the merged product of the previous $T-j$ runs that arrived since the last time Level $i$ was empty. Overall, an entry gets merged on average $\frac{T}{2}$, or $O(T)$, times per level before that level reaches capacity, and across $O(L)$ levels for a total of $O(T\cdot L)$ merge operations. As with tiering, we divide this by the block size $B$ to get the amortized I/O cost for one update: $O(\frac{L\cdot T}{B})$ I/O.

| Merge policy | Update cost | Point lookup cost | Short range lookup cost | Long range lookup cost |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Leveling | $O(\frac{L\cdot T}{B})$ | $O(e^{-M/N})$ | $O(L)$ | $O(\frac{s}{B})$ |
| Tiering | $O(\frac{L}{B})$ | $O(T\cdot e^{-M/N})$ | $O(L\cdot T)$ | $O(\frac{s\cdot T}{B})$ |

*Figure 3: In the worst-case, updates and short range lookups derive their I/O cost equally from access to all levels, whereas point lookups and long range lookups derive their I/O cost mostly from access to the largest level.*

We now take a closer look at how update cost derives from across different levels. With tiering, Level $i$ fills up every $B\cdot P\cdot T^i$ application updates, and the resulting merge operation copies $B\cdot P\cdot T^i$ entries. With leveling, a merge operation takes place at Level $i$ every $B\cdot P\cdot T^{i-1}$ updates (i.e., every time that a new run comes in), and it copies on average $\frac{B\cdot P\cdot T^i}{2}$ entries. By dividing the number of copied entries by the frequency of a merge operation at Level $i$ for either leveling or tiering, we observe that in the long run the amount of work done by merge operations at every level is the same, as shown with the cost breakdown in Figure 3 (A). The intuition is that while merge operations at larger levels do exponentially more work, they are also exponentially less frequent.

**Analyzing Point Lookups.** To analyze the worst-case point lookup cost, we focus on zero-result point lookups (i.e., to non-existing keys) because they maximize the long-run average of wasted I/Os (i.e., to runs that do not contain a target key). Zero-result point lookups are common in practice (e.g., in insert-if-not-exist operations) [16, 48], and we extend their analysis to point lookups that target existing entries in the next section.

The I/O cost for a zero-result point lookup is highest when every Bloom filter returns a false positive. In this case, a point lookup issues one I/O to every run, amounting to $O(L)$ wasted I/Os with leveling and $O(T\cdot L)$ wasted I/Os with tiering. In practice, however, the Bloom filters eliminate most I/Os to runs that do not contain the target key; key-value stores in industry use 10 bits per entry for every Bloom filter leading to a false positive rate (FPR) of $\approx 1\%$ for each filter [29, 32, 34]. For this reason, we focus on the *expected worst-case* point lookup cost, which estimates the number of I/Os issued by point lookups as a long-run average with respect to the Bloom filters’ FPRs. We estimate this cost as the sum of FPRs across all the Bloom filters. The reason is that the I/O cost of probing an individual run is an independent random variable with an expected value equal to the corresponding Bloom filter’s FPR, and the expected sum of multiple independent random variables is equal to the sum of their individual expected values [44].

In key-value stores in industry, the number of bits per entry for Bloom filters across all levels is the same [7, 29, 32, 34, 48]. Therefore, the Bloom filter(s) at the largest level are larger than the filters at all smaller levels combined, as they represent exponentially more entries. Thus, the FPR $p_L$ at the largest level is upper bounded using Equation 2 to $O(e^{-M/N})$, where $M$ is the memory budget for all filters and $N$ is the number of entries in the system. Since all other FPRs are the same as $p_L$, the expected point lookup cost is the product of $p_L$ and the number of runs in the system: $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot L)$ I/Os with leveling and $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot L\cdot T)$ I/Os with tiering.

The most recent paper on this issue named Monkey [22] shows that setting the same number of bits per entry for filters across all levels does not minimize the expected number of wasted I/Os. Instead, Monkey reallocates $\approx 1$ bit per entry from the filter(s) at the largest level, and it uses these bits to set the number of bits per entry across smaller levels as an increasing arithmetic progression: Level $i$ gets $a+b\cdot(L-i)$ bits per entry, where $a$ and $b$ are small constants. This causes a small, asymptotically constant increase to the FPR at the largest level and an exponential decrease to the FPRs across smaller levels, as they contain exponentially less entries. Since the FPRs are exponentially decreasing for smaller levels, the sum of FPRs converges to a multiplicative constant that is independent of the number of levels. As a result, Monkey shaves a factor of $L$ from the complexity of point lookups leading to $O(e^{-M/N})$ I/Os with leveling and $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot T)$ I/Os with tiering, as we illustrate in Figure 3 (B). It is always beneficial to use Monkey, for zero and non-zero result point lookups alike and with any kind of skew [22].

Overall, we observe that point lookup cost using Monkey derives mostly from the largest level, as smaller levels have exponentially lower FPRs and so access to them is exponentially less probable. As we will see at the end of this section, this opens up avenues for further optimization.

**Analyzing Space-Amplification.** We define space-amplification as the factor $amp$ by which the overall number of entries $N$ is greater than the number of unique entries $unq$: $amp=\frac{N}{unq}-1$. To analyze the worst-case space-amplification, we observe that Levels 1 to $L-1$ of LSM-tree comprise a fraction of $\frac{1}{T}$ of its capacity whereas Level $L$ comprises the remaining fraction of $\frac{T-1}{T}$ of its capacity. With leveling, the worst-case space-amplification occurs when entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ are all updates to different entries at Level $L$ thereby rendering at most a fraction of $\frac{1}{T}$ entries at Level $L$ obsolete.⁴ Space-amplification is therefore $O(\frac{1}{T})$, as shown in Figure 4 (A). For example, in production environments using SSDs at Facebook, RocksDB uses leveling and a size ratio of 10 to bound space-amplification to $\approx 10\%$ [27]. With tiering, the worst-case occurs when entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ are all updates to different entries at Level $L$, and where every run at Level $L$ contains the same set of entries. In this case, Level $L$ entirely consists of obsolete entries, and so space-amplification is $O(T)$ as Level $L$ is larger by a factor of $T-1$ than all other levels combined. Overall, space-amplification with both leveling and tiering in the worst case derives mostly from the presence of obsolete entries at the largest level.

⁴ In fact, if the largest level is not filled to capacity, space-amplification may be higher than $\frac{1}{T}$, but it is straightforward to dynamically enforce the size ratio across levels to $T$ during runtime by adjusting the capacities at Levels 1 to $L-1$ to guarantee the upper bound of $O(\frac{1}{T})$ as in RocksDB [27].

*Figure 4: Space-amplification is $O(\frac{1}{T})$ with leveling and $O(T)$ with tiering.*

**Analyzing Range Lookups.** We denote the selectivity of a range lookup $s$ as the number of unique entries across all runs that fall within the target key range. A range lookup scans and sort-merges the target key range across all runs, and it eliminates obsolete entries from the result set. For analysis, we consider a range lookup to be *long* if the number of blocks accessed is at least twice as large as the maximum possible number of levels: $\frac{s}{B}>2\cdot L_{max}$. Under uniformly randomly distributed updates, this condition implies with a high probability that most entries within a target key range are at the largest level. For all practical purposes, we generalize the treatment of long and short range lookups in Section 4.2.

A short range lookup issues approximately one I/O to every run amounting to $O(T\cdot L)$ I/Os with tiering and $O(L)$ I/Os with leveling, as shown in Figure 3 (C). For a long range lookup, the size of the result set before eliminating obsolete entries is on average the product of its selectivity and space-amplification. We divide this product by the block size to get the I/O cost: $O(\frac{T\cdot s}{B})$ with tiering and $O(\frac{s}{B})$ with leveling, as shown in Figure 3 (D).

A key distinction is that a short range lookup derives its cost approximately equally from across all levels, whereas a long range lookup derives most of its cost from access to the largest level.

**Mapping the Design Space to the Trade-Off Space.** There is an intrinsic trade-off between update cost on one hand and the costs of lookups and space-amplification on the other. We illustrate this trade-off in conceptual Figure 5, whereon the solid line plots the different costs of lookups and space-amplification on the y-axis against update cost on the x-axis for both leveling and tiering as we vary the size ratio, all based on the properties in Figures 3 and 4. When the size ratio is set to its limiting value of $T_{lim}$ (meaning there is only one level in storage), a tiered LSM-tree degenerates into a log whereas a leveled LSM-tree degenerates into a sorted array. When the size ratio is set to its lower limit of 2, the performance characteristics for leveling and tiering converge as their behaviors become identical: the number of levels is the same and a merge operation is triggered at every level when the second run comes in. In general, as the size ratio increases with leveling/tiering, lookup cost and space-amplification decrease/increase and update cost increases/decreases. Thus, the trade-off space is partitioned: leveling has strictly better lookup costs and space-amplification and strictly worse update cost than tiering.

*Figure 5: How far down is it possible to push the curve of possible space-time trade-offs?*

**The Holy Grail.** The solid line in Figure 5 reflects the properties of Monkey, the current state of the art. Figure 5 also shows a dotted line labeled the *elusive optimal*. The question guiding our research is whether other designs are possible with space-time trade-offs that more closely approach or even reach the elusive optimal.

**The Opportunity: Removing Superfluous Merging.** We have identified an asymmetry: point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and space-amplification derive mostly from the largest level, while update cost derives equally from across all levels. This means that merge operations at smaller levels significantly amplify update cost while yielding a comparatively insignificant benefit for space-amplification, point lookups, and long range lookups. There is therefore an opportunity of a merge policy that merges less at smaller levels.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们现在分析：在不同合并策略和大小比率下，最坏情况下的空间放大以及更新与查询的 I/O 成本如何来源于不同层级。为了分析更新和查询，我们采用磁盘访问模型 [1] 统计每次操作的 I/O 次数，其中一次 I/O 是指从二级存储读取或向其写入一个块。结果汇总于图 3 和图 4。

**更新分析。** 更新一条记录的 I/O 成本，是通过该更新记录随后参与的合并操作付出的。我们的分析假设一种最坏情况工作负载，其中所有更新都以最大层级中的记录为目标。这意味着，一条过时记录只有等到与之对应的更新记录到达最大层级后才会被移除。因此，每条记录都会跨所有层级进行合并（而不是在某个较小层级被更新的记录丢弃，从而降低后续合并操作的开销）。

采用分层式（tiering）策略时，一条记录在每个层级被合并 $O(1)$ 次，共经过 $O(L)$ 个层级。我们将其除以块大小 $B$，因为合并操作中的每次 I/O 都会把 $B$ 条记录从原有有序游程复制到新的 有序游程。因此，一次更新的摊还 I/O 成本为 $O(\frac{L}{B})$ 次 I/O。采用分级式（leveling）策略时，到达第 $i$ 层的第 $j$ 个有序游程会触发一次合并操作，其中包含第 $i$ 层已有的 有序游程；这个已有有序游程是自上一次第 $i$ 层为空以来，先前到达的 $T-j$ 个有序游程的合并结果。总体而言，在该层达到容量上限之前，一条记录在每层平均会被合并 $\frac{T}{2}$ 次，即 $O(T)$ 次；跨越 $O(L)$ 个层级，总计参与 $O(T\cdot L)$ 次合并操作。与分层式（tiering）一样，将其除以块大小 $B$，即可得到一次更新的摊还 I/O 成本：$O(\frac{L\cdot T}{B})$ 次 I/O。

| 合并策略 | 更新成本 | 点查询成本 | 短范围查询成本 | 长范围查询成本 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
|分级式（leveling）| $O(\frac{L\cdot T}{B})$ | $O(e^{-M/N})$ | $O(L)$ | $O(\frac{s}{B})$ |
|分层式（tiering）| $O(\frac{L}{B})$ | $O(T\cdot e^{-M/N})$ | $O(L\cdot T)$ | $O(\frac{s\cdot T}{B})$ |

*图 3：在最坏情况下，更新和短范围查询的 I/O 成本均等地来源于对所有层级的访问，而点查询和长范围查询的 I/O 成本主要来源于对最大层级的访问。*

下面进一步考察更新成本如何来源于不同层级。采用分层式（tiering）策略时，第 $i$ 层每经过 $B\cdot P\cdot T^i$ 次应用更新就会被填满，由此产生的合并操作会复制 $B\cdot P\cdot T^i$ 条记录。采用分级式（leveling）策略时，第 $i$ 层每经过 $B\cdot P\cdot T^{i-1}$ 次更新就会发生一次合并操作（即每当一个新有序游程到来时），该操作平均复制 $\frac{B\cdot P\cdot T^i}{2}$ 条记录。对于分级式（leveling）和分层式（tiering）中的任一种策略，将复制的记录数除以第 $i$ 层合并操作的发生频率，可以发现从长期来看，每个层级上合并操作完成的工作量相同，如图 3（A）的成本分解所示。其直觉在于：较大层级上的合并操作虽然要完成指数级更多的工作，但其发生频率也呈指数级降低。

**点查询分析。** 为了分析最坏情况下的点查询成本，我们关注零结果点查询（即查询不存在的键），因为它们会使浪费的 I/O（即访问不包含目标键的 有序游程）的长期平均值达到最大。零结果点查询在实践中很常见（例如在“若不存在则插入”操作中）[16, 48]；下一节还会将该分析扩展到以现有记录为目标的点查询。

当每个布隆过滤器都返回假阳性时，零结果点查询的 I/O 成本最高。在这种情况下，点查询会对每个有序游程发出一次 I/O；采用分级式（leveling）时会浪费 $O(L)$ 次 I/O，采用分层式（tiering）时会浪费 $O(T\cdot L)$ 次 I/O。不过在实践中，布隆过滤器能够消除绝大多数对不包含目标键的有序游程的 I/O；工业界键值存储通常为每个布隆过滤器中的每条记录分配 10 位，使每个过滤器的假阳性率（FPR）约为 $\approx 1\%$ [29, 32, 34]。因此，我们关注点查询的*期望最坏情况*成本，即根据布隆过滤器的 FPR，以长期平均值估计点查询发出的 I/O 次数。我们把这一成本估计为所有布隆过滤器 FPR 之和。原因是：探测单个有序游程的 I/O 成本是一个独立随机变量，其期望值等于相应布隆过滤器的 FPR；多个独立随机变量之和的期望，等于它们各自期望值之和 [44]。

在工业界键值存储中，所有层级的布隆过滤器为每条记录分配的位数相同 [7, 29, 32, 34, 48]。由于最大层级表示的记录数呈指数级增加，其布隆过滤器的总大小要大于所有较小层级过滤器之和。因此，根据公式 2，最大层级的 FPR $p_L$ 的上界为 $O(e^{-M/N})$，其中 $M$ 是所有过滤器的内存预算，$N$ 是系统中的记录数。由于其他所有 FPR 都与 $p_L$ 相同，期望点查询成本就是 $p_L$ 与系统中有序游程数量的乘积：分级式（leveling） 为 $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot L)$ 次 I/O，分层式（tiering） 为 $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot L\cdot T)$ 次 I/O。

关于这一问题的最新论文 Monkey [22] 表明，在所有层级的过滤器中为每条记录设置相同位数，并不能使浪费的 I/O 期望数量最小。相反，Monkey 从最大层级的过滤器中为每条记录重新分配约 $\approx 1$ 位，并利用这些位，使较小层级中每条记录的位数构成递增的等差数列：第 $i$ 层的每条记录获得 $a+b\cdot(L-i)$ 位，其中 $a$ 和 $b$ 是较小的常数。这样会使最大层级的 FPR 产生一个较小且渐近为常数的增量，同时使较小层级的 FPR 呈指数级下降，因为这些层级包含的记录数也呈指数级减少。由于越小层级的 FPR 越呈指数级下降，FPR 之和会收敛到一个与层数无关的乘法常数。因此，Monkey 从点查询复杂度中消去了一个因子 $L$，使分级式（leveling）下的成本变为 $O(e^{-M/N})$ 次 I/O，分层式（tiering） 下变为 $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot T)$ 次 I/O，如图 3（B）所示。无论点查询是零结果还是非零结果，也无论存在何种偏斜，使用 Monkey 总是有益的 [22]。

总体而言，我们观察到，采用 Monkey 时，点查询成本主要来源于最大层级，因为较小层级的 FPR 呈指数级降低，因而访问它们的概率也呈指数级下降。正如本节末尾将要看到的，这为进一步优化提供了机会。

**空间放大分析。** 我们把空间放大定义为：总记录数 $N$ 超过唯一记录数 $unq$ 的倍数 $amp$，即 $amp=\frac{N}{unq}-1$。为了分析最坏情况下的空间放大，我们注意到，LSM 树的第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层占其总容量的 $\frac{1}{T}$，而第 $L$ 层占余下的 $\frac{T-1}{T}$。采用分级式（leveling）策略时，最坏的空间放大发生在第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层中的记录全都是对第 $L$ 层不同记录的更新时，由此最多会使第 $L$ 层中 $\frac{1}{T}$ 的记录变为过时记录。⁴ 因此，空间放大为 $O(\frac{1}{T})$，如图 4（A）所示。例如，在 Facebook 使用 SSD 的生产环境中，RocksDB 采用分级式（leveling）和大小比率 10，把空间放大限制在约 $\approx 10\%$ [27]。采用分层式（tiering）策略时，最坏情况发生在第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的记录全都是对第 $L$ 层不同记录的更新，并且第 $L$ 层的每个有序游程都包含同一组记录时。在这种情况下，第 $L$ 层完全由过时记录构成；由于第 $L$ 层比其他所有层级之和大 $T-1$ 倍，空间放大为 $O(T)$。总体而言，在最坏情况下，分级式（leveling） 和分层式（tiering）的空间放大都主要源于最大层级中存在的过时记录。

⁴ 实际上，如果最大层级尚未填满，空间放大可能高于 $\frac{1}{T}$；不过，可以像 RocksDB [27] 那样，在运行时调整第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的容量，动态地把层间大小比率强制维持为 $T$，从而保证 $O(\frac{1}{T})$ 的上界。

*图 4：采用分级式（leveling）时空间放大为 $O(\frac{1}{T})$，采用分层式（tiering）时为 $O(T)$。*

**范围查询分析。** 我们用范围查询的选择率 $s$ 表示所有有序游程中落入目标键范围的唯一记录数量。范围查询会扫描所有有序游程中的目标键范围并对其进行归并排序，同时从结果集中消除过时记录。在分析中，如果访问的块数至少为可能的最大层数的两倍，即 $\frac{s}{B}>2\cdot L_{max}$，我们就把该范围查询视为*长范围查询*。当更新均匀随机分布时，这一条件意味着目标键范围中的大多数记录很可能位于最大层级。为了覆盖所有实际情况，我们将在第 4.2 节中推广对长、短范围查询的处理方法。

短范围查询大约会对每个有序游程发出一次 I/O，因此采用分层式（tiering）时需要 $O(T\cdot L)$ 次 I/O，采用分级式（leveling）时需要 $O(L)$ 次 I/O，如图 3（C）所示。对于长范围查询，在消除过时记录之前，结果集大小平均等于其选择率与空间放大的乘积。把该乘积除以块大小即可得到 I/O 成本：分层式（tiering） 为 $O(\frac{T\cdot s}{B})$，分级式（leveling） 为 $O(\frac{s}{B})$，如图 3（D）所示。

一个关键区别是：短范围查询的成本大致均等地来源于所有层级，而长范围查询的大部分成本则来源于对最大层级的访问。

**从设计空间映射到权衡空间。** 更新成本与查询成本及空间放大之间存在内在权衡。概念图 5 展示了这种权衡：基于图 3 和图 4 中的性质，在改变大小比率时，实线以 x 轴表示更新成本，以 y 轴表示分级式（leveling）和分层式（tiering）下不同的查询成本与空间放大。当大小比率被设为其极限值 $T_{lim}$（即存储中只有一个层级）时，采用分层式（tiering）的 LSM 树会退化为日志，而采用分级式（leveling）的 LSM 树会退化为有序数组。当大小比率设为下限 2 时，分级式（leveling） 与分层式（tiering）的性能特征会收敛，因为二者的行为变得相同：层数相同，并且每个层级都在第二个有序游程到达时触发合并操作。一般而言，随着 分级式（leveling）/分层式（tiering） 的大小比率增大，查询成本和空间放大分别降低/增大，而更新成本分别增大/降低。因此，权衡空间被分割开来：与分层式（tiering）相比，分级式（leveling） 的查询成本和空间放大严格更优，但更新成本严格更差。

*图 5：可能的时空权衡曲线究竟还能向下推进多远？*

**终极目标。** 图 5 中的实线反映了当前最先进方法 Monkey 的性质。图 5 还给出了一条标为“难以企及的最优值”的虚线。指导我们研究的问题是：是否存在其他设计，其时空权衡能更接近、甚至达到这一难以企及的最优值。

**机会：消除多余合并。** 我们发现了一种不对称性：点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大主要来自最大层级，而更新成本则均等地来自所有层级。这意味着，较小层级的合并操作会显著放大更新成本，但对降低空间放大、点查询和长范围查询成本的帮助相对微不足道。因此，这里存在一种机会：设计一种在较小层级减少合并的合并策略。

sections.title: 4 LAZY LEVELING, FLUID LSM-TREE, AND DOSTOEVSKY
sections.titleTranslation: 4 惰性分级式、流式 LSM 树与 Dostoevsky
sections.originalMarkdown: We now present Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-Tree, and Dostoevsky to fluidly and dynamically adapt across an expanded LSM-tree design space with richer performance and space trade-offs.
sections.translationMarkdown: 下面介绍 惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）、流式 LSM 树（Fluid LSM-Tree） 和 Dostoevsky；它们能够在扩展后的 LSM 树设计空间中流畅、动态地进行适配，从而提供更加丰富的性能与空间权衡。

sections.title: 4.1 Lazy Leveling
sections.originalMarkdown:
Lazy Leveling is a merge policy that eliminates merging at all but the largest level of LSM-tree. The motivation is that merging at these smaller levels significantly increases update cost while yielding a comparatively insignificant improvement for point lookups, long range lookups, and space-amplification. Relative to leveling, we show that Lazy Leveling (1) improves the cost complexity of updates, (2) maintains the same complexity for point lookups, long range lookups, and space-amplification, and (3) provides competitive performance for short range lookups. We summarize the structure and performance characteristics of Lazy Leveling in Figure 6, and we discuss this figure in detail in the rest of the section.

**Basic Structure.** The top part of Figure 6 illustrates the structure of Lazy Leveling and compares it to tiering and leveling. Lazy leveling at its core is a hybrid of leveling and tiering: it applies leveling at the largest level and tiering at all other levels. As a result, the number of runs at the largest level is 1 and the number of runs at all other levels is at most $T-1$ (i.e., a merge operation takes place when the $T$th run arrives).

**Bloom Filters Allocation.** Next, we show how to keep the cost complexity of point lookups fixed despite having more runs to probe at smaller levels. We do this by optimizing the memory budget among the Bloom filters across different levels. We start by modeling point lookup cost and the filters’ overall main memory footprint with respect to the FPRs.

| Characteristic | Tiering | Leveling | Lazy Leveling |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Runs per smaller level | $T-1$ | $1$ | $T-1$ |
| Runs at Level $L$ | $T-1$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| Zero-result point lookup cost | $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot T)$ | $O(e^{-M/N})$ | $O(e^{-M/N})$ |
| Point lookup cost to existing entries | $O(1+e^{-M/N}\cdot T)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Short range lookup cost | $O(L\cdot T)$ | $O(L)$ | $O(1+(L-1)\cdot T)$ |
| Long range lookup cost | $O(\frac{T\cdot s}{B})$ | $O(\frac{s}{B})$ | $O(\frac{s}{B})$ |
| Update cost | $O(\frac{L}{B})$ | $O(\frac{L\cdot T}{B})$ | $O(\frac{L+T}{B})$ |
| Space-amplification | $O(T)$ | $O(\frac{1}{T})$ | $O(\frac{1}{T})$ |

*Figure 6: A cost breakdown of updates, lookups, and space-amplification with different merge policies. Lazy Leveling achieves the same space-amplification, point lookup cost, and long range lookup cost as leveling while improving update cost and providing a slightly worse yet competitive short range lookup cost.*

The worst-case expected number of wasted I/Os per lookup is issued by a zero-result point lookup and is equal to the sum of false positive rates across every run’s Bloom filters. We model this cost for Lazy Leveling in Equation 3. The additive term $p_L$ corresponds to the FPR for the single run at Level $L$, and the other term sums up the products of FPRs and number of runs at Levels 1 to $L-1$.

$$
R=p_L+(T-1)\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L-1}p_i \quad \text{where }0<p_i<1
\tag{3}
$$

Next, we model the memory footprint $M_i$ for the Bloom filters at Level $i$ with respect to the number of entries $N_i$ and the FPR $p_i$ at that level. We do this by rearranging Equation 2 in terms of bits and applying it to each level. Since the filters at any given level all have the same FPR, we can directly apply this equation regardless of the numbers of runs at a level. The result is $M_i=-N_i\cdot\frac{\ln(p_i)}{\ln(2)^2}$. Next, we express $N_i$ more generally as the product of the capacity at the largest level $N\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}$ and a discounting factor to adjust for the capacity at Level $i$: $\frac{1}{T^{L-i}}$. We then sum up the memory footprint across all levels to get the overall memory footprint $M$. The result is Equation 4.

$$
M=-\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L}\frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}}
\tag{4}
$$

We now optimize Equations 3 and 4 with respect to each other to find the FPRs that minimize point lookup cost $R$ with respect to a given memory budget $M$. To do so, we use the method of Lagrange multipliers. The full derivation is in Appendix A. The result is Equation 5.

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
R\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}, & \text{for }i=L\\
R\cdot\frac{1}{T^{L-i+1}}, & \text{for }1\le i<L
\end{cases}
\tag{5}
$$

**Zero-Result Point Lookups.** Next, we analyze the cost of zero-result point lookups $R$ with Lazy Leveling. We plug the optimal FPRs from Equation 5 into Equation 4, simplify into closed-form, and rearrange in terms of $R$. The complete derivation is in Appendix B. The result is Equation 6.

$$
R=e^{-\frac{M}{N}\cdot\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T^{\frac{T}{T-1}}}{(T-1)^{\frac{T-1}{T}}}
\tag{6}
$$

This equation allows to quickly find the optimal FPRs with respect to a given memory budget $M$ by plugging in the corresponding value of $R$ from Equation 6 into Equation 5.

To analyze the complexity of zero-result point lookups, we observe that the multiplicative term at the right-hand side of Equation 6 is a small constant for any value of $T$. Therefore, the cost complexity is $O(e^{-M/N})$, the same as with leveling despite having eliminated most merge operations.

**Memory Requirement.** As the number of entries $N$ grows relative to the memory budget $M$, the FPRs increase and eventually converge to one (starting from larger to smaller levels because the FPR at larger levels is higher). We identify the ratio of bits per entry $M/N$ at which point the FPR at Level $L$ converges to one by plugging in one for $p_L$ in Equation 5, plugging the corresponding value of $R$ into Equation 6, and rearranging in terms of $\frac{M}{N}$.

$$
\text{threshold for }\frac{M}{N}=\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\left(\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}+\frac{\ln(T-1)}{T}\right)
\tag{7}
$$

Equation 7 has global maximum of $M/N=1.62$ bits per entry (which occurs when $T$ is set to 3). For mainstream key-value stores used for server applications, the default ratio is an order of magnitude larger, typically 10 [29, 32, 48] or 16 [52], and so the FPRs are all lower than one. For systems with less than 1.62 bits per entry (e.g., mobile devices or sensors), we adapt Lazy Leveling and its analysis in Appendix C by merging more at larger levels.

**Point Lookups for Existing Entries.** The worst-case point lookup cost to an existing entry occurs when the target key is at Level $L$. The expected I/O cost comprises one I/O to Level $L$ plus the sum of FPRs across all other levels (i.e., $R-p_L$) and is given in Equation 8. The cost complexity is $O(1)$ I/Os because the term $R-p_L$ is always less than 1 as long as the memory requirement in Equation 7 holds.

$$
V=1+R-p_L
\tag{8}
$$

**Range Lookups.** A short range lookup issues at most $O(T)$ I/Os to each of the first $L-1$ levels and one I/O to the largest level, and so the cost complexity is $O(1+(L-1)\cdot T)$ I/Os. Note that this expression initially increases as $T$ increases, but as $T$ approaches its limiting value of $T_{lim}$ this term converges to 1 as the additive term $(L-1)\cdot T$ on the right-hand size becomes zero (i.e., at this point Lazy Leveling degenerates into a sorted array).

A long range lookup is dominated by sequential access to Level $L$ because it contains exponentially more entries than all other levels. The cost is $O(\frac{s}{B})$ I/Os, where $s$ is the size of the target key range relative to the size of the existing key space. This is the same as with leveling despite having eliminated most merge operations.

**Updates.** An updated entry with Lazy Leveling participates in $O(1)$ merge operations per level across Levels 1 to $L-1$ and in $O(T)$ merge operations at Level $L$. The overall number of merge operations per entry is therefore $O(L+T)$, and we divide it by the block size $B$ to get the cost for a single update: $O(\frac{L+T}{B})$. This is an improvement over the worst-case cost with leveling.

**Space-Amplification.** In the worst case, every entry at Levels 1 to $L-1$ is an update to an existing entry at Level $L$. Since the fraction of entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ is $\frac{1}{T}$ of the overall number of entries, space-amplification is at most $O(\frac{1}{T})$. This is the same bound as with leveling despite having eliminated most merge operations.

*Figure 7: Lazy Leveling offers better trade-offs between updates and point lookups (Part A), worse trade-offs between updates and short range lookups (Part B), and the same trade-offs between updates and long range lookups (Part C).*

**Limits.** Figure 7 compares the behaviors of the different merge policies as we vary the size ratio $T$ for each policy from 2 to its limit of $T_{lim}$ (i.e., at which point the number of levels drops to one). Firstly, we observe that these policies converge in terms of performance characteristics when the size ratio $T$ is set to 2 because at this point their behaviors become identical: the number of levels is the same and a merge operation occurs at every level when the second run arrives. Secondly, Part (A) of Figure 7 shows that the improvement that Lazy Leveling achieves for update cost relative to leveling can be traded for point lookup cost by increasing the size ratio. This generates a new trade-off curve between update cost and point lookup cost that dominates leveling, and converges with it again as $T$ approaches $T_{lim}$ (i.e., at which point both merge policies degenerate into a sorted array). Parts (B) shows that the cost of small range lookups is competitive, and Part (C) shows that this cost difference becomes negligible as the target range grows.

**Lesson: No Single Merge Policy Rules.** Our analysis in figure Figure 7 shows that no single design dominates the others universally. Lazy Leveling is best for combined workloads consisting of updates, point lookups and long range lookups, whereas tiering and leveling are best for workloads comprising mostly updates or mostly lookups, respectively. In the rest of the paper, we take steps towards a unified system that adapts across these designs depending on the application scenario.
sections.translationMarkdown:
惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling） 是一种除 LSM 树最大层级外、在其他所有层级消除合并的合并策略。其动机在于，较小层级的合并会显著增加更新成本，但对点查询、长范围查询和空间放大的改善相对微不足道。与分级式（leveling）相比，我们证明 惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）：（1）改善了更新的成本复杂度；（2）保持点查询、长范围查询和空间放大的复杂度不变；（3）为短范围查询提供有竞争力的性能。图 6 汇总了惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）的结构和性能特征，本节余下部分将详细讨论该图。

**基本结构。** 图 6 上部展示了惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）的结构，并将其与分层式（tiering）和分级式（leveling）进行了比较。惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling） 的核心是分级式（leveling）与分层式（tiering）的混合体：它在最大层级采用 分级式（leveling），在其他所有层级采用 分层式（tiering）。因此，最大层级有 1 个 有序游程，而其他每个层级至多有 $T-1$ 个 有序游程（即第 $T$ 个有序游程到达时发生一次合并操作）。

**布隆过滤器分配。** 接下来，我们说明：尽管较小层级中需要探测的有序游程更多，如何仍能保持点查询的成本复杂度不变。为此，我们优化不同层级的布隆过滤器之间的内存预算分配。首先，根据 FPR 对点查询成本和过滤器的总主存占用进行建模。

| 特征 |分层式（tiering）|分级式（leveling）|惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）|
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| 每个较小层级的有序游程数 | $T-1$ | $1$ | $T-1$ |
| 第 $L$ 层的有序游程数 | $T-1$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| 零结果点查询成本 | $O(e^{-M/N}\cdot T)$ | $O(e^{-M/N})$ | $O(e^{-M/N})$ |
| 查询现有记录的点查询成本 | $O(1+e^{-M/N}\cdot T)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| 短范围查询成本 | $O(L\cdot T)$ | $O(L)$ | $O(1+(L-1)\cdot T)$ |
| 长范围查询成本 | $O(\frac{T\cdot s}{B})$ | $O(\frac{s}{B})$ | $O(\frac{s}{B})$ |
| 更新成本 | $O(\frac{L}{B})$ | $O(\frac{L\cdot T}{B})$ | $O(\frac{L+T}{B})$ |
| 空间放大 | $O(T)$ | $O(\frac{1}{T})$ | $O(\frac{1}{T})$ |

*图 6：不同合并策略下更新、查询和空间放大的成本分解。惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling） 在改善更新成本的同时，实现了与分级式（leveling）相同的空间放大、点查询成本和长范围查询成本，并提供了稍差但仍有竞争力的短范围查询成本。*

每次查询所浪费 I/O 数量的最坏期望值由零结果点查询产生，它等于每个有序游程的布隆过滤器的假阳性率之和。公式 3 对惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）的这一成本进行了建模。加法项 $p_L$ 对应第 $L$ 层单个有序游程的 FPR，另一项则对第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的 FPR 与有序游程数量的乘积求和。

$$
R=p_L+(T-1)\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L-1}p_i \quad \text{其中 }0<p_i<1
\tag{3}
$$

接下来，我们根据第 $i$ 层的记录数 $N_i$ 和该层的 FPR $p_i$，对第 $i$ 层布隆过滤器的内存占用 $M_i$ 进行建模。具体做法是把公式 2 按位数重新整理，并将其应用于每一层。由于任一给定层级中的所有过滤器都具有相同的 FPR，因此无论该层有多少个 有序游程，都可以直接应用这一公式。结果为 $M_i=-N_i\cdot\frac{\ln(p_i)}{\ln(2)^2}$。随后，我们以更一般的形式表示 $N_i$：它等于最大层级的容量 $N\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}$ 与一个折减因子的乘积，该折减因子用于根据第 $i$ 层的容量进行调整，即 $\frac{1}{T^{L-i}}$。然后，对所有层级的内存占用求和，得到总内存占用 $M$，结果如公式 4 所示。

$$
M=-\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L}\frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}}
\tag{4}
$$

现在，我们将公式 3 和公式 4 相互约束地进行优化，以找出在给定内存预算 $M$ 下使点查询成本 $R$ 最小的各层 FPR。为此，我们采用拉格朗日乘子法。完整推导见附录 A，结果如公式 5 所示。

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
R\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}, & \text{当 }i=L\\
R\cdot\frac{1}{T^{L-i+1}}, & \text{当 }1\le i<L
\end{cases}
\tag{5}
$$

**零结果点查询。** 接下来分析惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）下零结果点查询的成本 $R$。我们把公式 5 中的最优 FPR 代入公式 4，将其化简为闭式表达式，并按 $R$ 重新整理。完整推导见附录 B，结果如公式 6 所示。

$$
R=e^{-\frac{M}{N}\cdot\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T^{\frac{T}{T-1}}}{(T-1)^{\frac{T-1}{T}}}
\tag{6}
$$

对于给定的内存预算 $M$，把公式 6 中相应的 $R$ 值代入公式 5，即可利用该公式快速求出最优 FPR。

为了分析零结果点查询的复杂度，我们注意到，对于任意 $T$ 值，公式 6 右侧的乘法项都是一个较小的常数。因此，其成本复杂度为 $O(e^{-M/N})$；尽管消除了大部分合并操作，该复杂度仍与分级式（leveling）相同。

**内存需求。** 随着记录数 $N$ 相对于内存预算 $M$ 增长，FPR 会增大并最终收敛到 1（从较大层级开始，逐步扩展到较小层级，因为较大层级的 FPR 更高）。为了确定第 $L$ 层 FPR 收敛到 1 时每条记录的位数比率 $M/N$，我们在公式 5 中令 $p_L=1$，把对应的 $R$ 值代入公式 6，再按 $\frac{M}{N}$ 重新整理。

$$
\frac{M}{N}\text{ 的阈值}=\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\left(\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}+\frac{\ln(T-1)}{T}\right)
\tag{7}
$$

公式 7 的全局最大值为每条记录 $M/N=1.62$ 位（在 $T=3$ 时取得）。对于服务器应用所使用的主流键值存储，默认比率要高一个数量级，通常为 10 [29, 32, 48] 或 16 [52]，因此所有 FPR 均小于 1。对于每条记录少于 1.62 位的系统（例如移动设备或传感器），我们通过在较大层级执行更多合并，对惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）及其分析作出调整，详见附录 C。

**对现有记录的点查询。** 查询现有记录的最坏点查询成本发生在目标键位于第 $L$ 层时。期望 I/O 成本由访问第 $L$ 层的一次 I/O 加上其他所有层级的 FPR 之和（即 $R-p_L$）构成，如公式 8 所示。只要满足公式 7 的内存需求，$R-p_L$ 项就始终小于 1，因此成本复杂度为 $O(1)$ 次 I/O。

$$
V=1+R-p_L
\tag{8}
$$

**范围查询。** 短范围查询对前 $L-1$ 个层级中的每一层至多发出 $O(T)$ 次 I/O，并对最大层级发出一次 I/O，因此成本复杂度为 $O(1+(L-1)\cdot T)$ 次 I/O。需要注意，随着 $T$ 增大，该表达式起初会增大；但当 $T$ 接近其极限值 $T_{lim}$ 时，由于右侧加法项 $(L-1)\cdot T$ 变为零，该项会收敛到 1（此时惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）退化为有序数组）。

由于第 $L$ 层包含的记录数比其他所有层级呈指数级更多，长范围查询的成本由对第 $L$ 层的顺序访问主导。其成本为 $O(\frac{s}{B})$ 次 I/O，其中 $s$ 是目标键范围大小相对于现有键空间大小的比例。尽管消除了大部分合并操作，这一成本仍与分级式（leveling）相同。

**更新。** 在惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）中，一条更新记录在第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的每一层参与 $O(1)$ 次合并操作，并在第 $L$ 层参与 $O(T)$ 次合并操作。因此，每条记录参与合并操作的总次数为 $O(L+T)$；将其除以块大小 $B$，即可得到单次更新的成本：$O(\frac{L+T}{B})$。这优于分级式（leveling）的最坏情况成本。

**空间放大。** 在最坏情况下，第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层中的每条记录都是对第 $L$ 层现有记录的更新。由于第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的记录占总记录数的 $\frac{1}{T}$，空间放大至多为 $O(\frac{1}{T})$。尽管消除了大部分合并操作，这一上界仍与分级式（leveling）相同。

*图 7：惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling） 在更新与点查询之间提供更优的权衡（A 部分），在更新与短范围查询之间提供较差的权衡（B 部分），并在更新与长范围查询之间提供相同的权衡（C 部分）。*

**极限。** 图 7 比较了不同合并策略的行为：对于每种策略，把大小比率 $T$ 从 2 改变到其极限值 $T_{lim}$（此时层数降为 1）。首先可以观察到，当大小比率 $T$ 设为 2 时，这些策略的性能特征会收敛，因为此时它们的行为变得相同：层数相同，而且每一层都在第二个有序游程到达时发生合并操作。其次，图 7（A）表明，通过增大大小比率，可以把惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling）相对于分级式（leveling）在更新成本上的改进交换为点查询成本。这样会生成一条新的更新成本—点查询成本权衡曲线，它支配 分级式（leveling），并在 $T$ 接近 $T_{lim}$ 时再次与分级式（leveling）收敛（此时两种合并策略都退化为有序数组）。图 7（B）表明，小范围查询的成本具有竞争力；图 7（C）表明，随着目标范围增大，这一成本差异会变得可以忽略。

**启示：不存在一统天下的合并策略。** 图 7 中的分析表明，没有任何一种设计能够在所有情况下都支配其他设计。惰性分级式（Lazy Leveling） 最适合由更新、点查询和长范围查询组成的混合工作负载；分层式（tiering） 和分级式（leveling）则分别最适合主要由更新或主要由查询组成的工作负载。在本文余下部分，我们将逐步构建一个统一系统，使其能够根据应用场景在这些设计之间进行适配。

sections.title: 4.2 Fluid LSM-Tree
sections.originalMarkdown:
To be able to strike all possible trade-offs for different workloads, we next introduce Fluid LSM-tree, a generalization of LSM-tree that enables switching and combining merge policies. It does this by controlling the frequency of merge operations separately for the largest level and for all other levels.

**Basic Structure.** Figure 8 illustrates the basic structure of Fluid LSM-tree. There are at most $Z$ runs at the largest level and at most $K$ runs at each of the smaller levels. To maintain these bounds, every Level $i$ has an active run into which we merge incoming runs from Level $i-1$. Each active run has a size threshold with respect to the capacity of its level: $T/K$ percent for Levels 1 to $L-1$ and $T/Z$ percent for Level $L$. When an active run reaches this threshold, we start a new active run at that level. Ultimately when a level is at capacity, all runs in it get merged and flushed down to the next level.

**Parameterization.** The bounds $K$ and $Z$ are used as tuning parameters that enable Fluid LSM-tree to assume the behaviors of different merge policies.

- $K=1$ and $Z=1$ give leveling.
- $K=T-1$ and $Z=T-1$ give tiering.
- $K=T-1$ and $Z=1$ give Lazy Leveling.

Fluid LSM-tree can transition from Lazy Leveling to tiering by merging less frequently at the largest level by increasing $Z$, or it can transition to leveling by merging more frequently at all other levels by decreasing $K$. Fluid LSM-tree spans all possible trade-offs along and between the curves in Figure 7.

**Bloom Filters Allocation.** Next, we derive the optimal FPRs that minimize point lookup cost with respect to the parameters $K$ and $Z$. The derivation is in Appendix A and the result is Equation 9.

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{R}{Z}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}, & \text{for } i=L,\\[4pt]
\dfrac{R}{K}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T^{L-i}}, & \text{for } 1\le i<L.
\end{cases}
\tag{9}
$$

Equation 9 generalizes the optimal Bloom filters allocation strategy in Monkey [22] across a significantly wider design space, which now, in addition to tiering and leveling, also includes Lazy Leveling as well as custom merge policies with any parameter values for $K$ and $Z$. Next, we model and map the new space-time trade-offs that this expanded design space offers.

**Figure 8: A cost breakdown of updates, lookups, and space-amplification with Fluid LSM-tree.**

**Zero-Result Point Lookups.** We model the cost of zero-result point lookups by plugging the generalized optimal FPRs in Equation 9 into Equation 4, simplifying into closed-form, and rearranging in terms of $R$. The derivation is in Appendix B, experimental validation is in Appendix I, and the result is Equation 10. The generalized complexity is $O(Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$ I/Os.

$$
R=e^{-\frac{M}{N}\cdot\ln(2)^2}\cdot Z\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot K^{1/T}\cdot\frac{T^{T/(T-1)}}{T-1}.
\tag{10}
$$

**Point Lookups for Existing Entries.** The worst-case lookup cost to an existing key occurs when the target key is at the oldest run at the largest level. The expected I/O cost is one I/O to this target run plus the sum of FPRs across all other runs. We use Equation 8 to model this, and we plug in Equation 10 for $R$ and Equation 9 for $p_L$. The generalized complexity is $O(1+Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$.

**Memory Requirement.** In Appendix C, we derive the memory requirement $M/N$ that guarantees that FPRs across all Levels are lower than one. The generalized result is 1.62 bits per entry as in the last subsection, which is well below the default ratio in mainstream systems. In Appendix C, we show how to adapt Fluid LSM-tree to extremely low-memory environments.

**Range Lookups.** A short range lookup issues at most $K$ I/Os per level to the smaller $L-1$ Levels and at most $Z$ I/Os to the largest level for a total of $Z+K\cdot(L-1)$ random I/Os and a cost complexity of $O(Z+K\cdot(L-1))$. A long range lookup continues with a sequential scan to the relevant key range at each run issuing at least $s/B$ sequential I/Os, where $s$ is the number of unique entries in the target key range. To account for obsolete entries, the number of sequential I/Os is amplified by a factor of $1+1/T$ for updated entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ and $Z$ for updated entries at Level $L$, which we model together as $Z+1/T$. The sequential scan cost is therefore at most $s/B\cdot(Z+1/T)$ I/Os with a complexity of $O(s\cdot Z/B)$ I/Os. The generalized range lookup cost is given in Equation 11 as the sum of costs for short and long range lookups weighted by the constant $\mu$, the amount by which sequential access is faster than random access on a given storage devices (e.g., disk).

$$
Q=K\cdot(L-1)+Z+\frac{1}{\mu}\cdot\frac{s}{B}\cdot\left(Z+\frac{1}{T}\right).
\tag{11}
$$

**Updates.** In the worst case, an entry participates in $O(T/K)$ merge operations within an active run across each of Levels 1 to $L-1$, and in $O(T/Z)$ merge operations within the active run at Level $L$. The overall update cost is the sum of these terms across all levels divided by the block size: $O\left(\frac{T}{B}\cdot\left(\frac{L}{K}+\frac{1}{Z}\right)\right)$. We model this cost more precisely using arithmetic series to obtain Equation 12, which we validate in Appendix I. We divide by the constant $\mu$ since the cost of updates is incurred through sequential merge operations, and we introduce an additional constant $\phi$ to account for the property of some storage devices that writes are more expensive than reads (e.g., flash).

$$
W=\frac{\phi}{\mu\cdot B}\cdot\left(\frac{T-1}{K+1}\cdot(L-1)+\frac{T-1}{Z+1}\right).
\tag{12}
$$

**Space-Amplification.** Levels 1 to $L-1$ contain a fraction of $1/T$ of the dataset, and so they may render up to this fraction of entries obsolete at the largest level. In Level $L$, at most $Z-1$ of the runs may be completely filled with obsolete entries. We model space-amplification as the sum of these terms in Equation 13.

$$
\operatorname{amp}=Z-1+\frac{1}{T}.
\tag{13}
$$

**Mapping the Design Space.** Figure 9 is an instance of conceptual Figure 7 that uses our cost models to map the different trade-offs with Fluid LSM-tree. We generate Part (A) of Figure 9 by plotting point lookup cost $R$ in Equation 10 against update cost $W$ in Equation 12. We generate Parts (B) and (C) for short and long range lookups by plotting $Q$ in Equation 11 against update cost $W$ in Equation 12 for selectivities $s$ of $10^{-7}$ and $10^{-6}$, respectively. We leave an evaluation of space-amplification for the experimental analysis. The configuration is fixed to a 1TB dataset with 128 byte entries, 4KB storage blocks, and overall 10 bits per entry across the filters.⁵ We generate the curves for leveling, tiering, and Lazy Leveling by using their corresponding fixed values for the parameters $K$ and $Z$, and varying the size ratio $T$. The circle indicates the convergence point of all three merge policies where the size ratio $T$ is set to two. The squares indicate a size ratio of ten, which most mainstream key-value stores use by default in practice [29, 32], to enable comparison of corresponding points across the three sub-figures. The figure also illustrates two transition curves labeled Trans1 and Trans2, which demonstrate how Fluid LSM-tree transitions fluidly across designs thereby achieving trade-offs that would not have been possible using a fixed merge policy.

**Figure 9: Fluidly shifting between Lazy Leveling, leveling, and tiering.**

**Transition 1: Lazy Leveling to Tiering.** We observe in Part (A) of Figure 9 that the curve for Lazy Leveling has an inflection point beyond which decreasing the size ratio degrades update cost. The reason is that update cost is $O((L+T)/B)$, and as we decrease $T$ the value of $L$ grows and comes to dominate $T$. In this example, the inflection point occurs when the size ratio $T$ is set to 5. We generate the curve labeled Transition 1 (Trans1) by fixing $T$ to the inflection point value and instead varying $Z$ from 1 to $T-1$ (4 in this example). The resulting curve dominates both Lazy Leveling and tiering for this part of the design space until it converges with tiering. Thus, Transition 1 enables optimal trade-offs between point lookup cost and update cost as we transition between Lazy Leveling and tiering to optimize more for point lookups or updates, respectively.

**Transition 2: Lazy Leveling to Leveling.** In order to achieve more competitive range lookup costs with Lazy Leveling, we introduce Transition 2. The idea is to vary $K$, the bound on runs at Levels 1 to $L-1$, between 1 and $T-1$ to fluidly transition between Lazy Leveling and leveling. In Figure 9 we generate the curve labeled Trans2 by fixing $K$ to 4 and varying $T$. Part (A) shows that this enables navigating a trade-off curve similar to Lazy Leveling, and parts (B) and (C) show that Trans2 achieves nearly the same range lookup cost as with leveling. Thus, Transition 2 enables fine control over how much we optimize for short range lookups.

**Problem: Finding the Best Tuning.** The space of optimal space-time trade-offs is delineated by leveling, Lazy Leveling, and tiering, and we can reach many other trade-offs in-between by co-tuning the parameters $K$, $Z$ and $T$. The goal is to co-tune these parameters to strike the best trade-off for a particular application. The challenge is finding the optimal configuration as there are many different combinations for these parameters.⁶

⁵ We set $N=2^{33}$, $E=2^7$, $B=2^5$, and $M=10\cdot2^{33}$.

⁶ The number of combinations is $O((N/(B\cdot P))^3)$. The reason is that the maximum value of $T$ is $T_{lim}=N/(B\cdot P)$, and $K$ and $Z$ can both be tuned to anything between 1 and $T-1$.
sections.translationMarkdown:
为了能够针对不同工作负载实现所有可能的权衡，我们接下来引入流体 LSM 树（Fluid LSM-tree）。它是 LSM 树的一种泛化，允许切换和组合不同的合并策略。其实现方式是分别控制最大层与其他所有层的合并操作频率。

**基本结构。** 图 8 展示了流体 LSM 树的基本结构。最大层至多有 $Z$ 个有序游程，每个较小层至多有 $K$ 个有序游程。为了维持这些上界，每一层 $i$ 都有一个活跃有序游程，来自第 $i-1$ 层的有序游程会被合并到其中。每个活跃有序游程相对于其所在层容量都有一个大小阈值：第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层为 $T/K$，第 $L$ 层为 $T/Z$。当活跃有序游程达到该阈值时，我们在该层启动一个新的活跃有序游程。最终，当某一层达到容量上限时，其中的所有有序游程都会被合并并向下刷入下一层。

**参数化。** 上界 $K$ 和 $Z$ 被用作调优参数，使流体 LSM 树能够呈现不同合并策略的行为。

- $K=1$ 且 $Z=1$ 对应 分级式（leveling）。
- $K=T-1$ 且 $Z=T-1$ 对应 分层式（tiering）。
- $K=T-1$ 且 $Z=1$ 对应惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）。

通过增大 $Z$、降低最大层的合并频率，流体 LSM 树可以从惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）过渡到分层式（tiering）；通过减小 $K$、提高其他所有层的合并频率，它也可以过渡到分级式（leveling）。流体 LSM 树覆盖了图 7 中各条曲线上以及曲线之间的所有可能权衡。

**布隆过滤器分配。** 接下来，我们针对参数 $K$ 和 $Z$ 推导使点查询成本最小的最优假阳性率（FPR）。推导见附录 A，结果为公式 9。

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{R}{Z}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}, & \text{当 } i=L,\\[4pt]
\dfrac{R}{K}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T^{L-i}}, & \text{当 } 1\le i<L.
\end{cases}
\tag{9}
$$

公式 9 将 Monkey [22] 中的最优布隆过滤器分配策略推广到了显著更大的设计空间。这个空间除了分层式（tiering）和分级式（leveling）之外，现在还包括惰性分级（Lazy Leveling），以及 $K$ 和 $Z$ 可取任意参数值的定制合并策略。接下来，我们对这个扩展设计空间所提供的新时空权衡进行建模和映射。

**图 8：流体 LSM 树中更新、查找和空间放大成本的分解。**

**零结果点查询。** 我们将公式 9 中推广后的最优 FPR 代入公式 4，化为闭式表达式，并按 $R$ 重新整理，从而对零结果点查询的成本建模。推导见附录 B，实验验证见附录 I，结果为公式 10。推广后的复杂度为 $O(Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$ 次 I/O。

$$
R=e^{-\frac{M}{N}\cdot\ln(2)^2}\cdot Z\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot K^{1/T}\cdot\frac{T^{T/(T-1)}}{T-1}.
\tag{10}
$$

**对已有条目的点查询。** 对已有键的最坏情况查找发生在目标键位于最大层中最老的有序游程时。预期 I/O 成本是访问该目标有序游程的一次 I/O，再加上其他所有有序游程 FPR 的总和。我们使用公式 8 对此建模，并将公式 10 中的 $R$ 和公式 9 中的 $p_L$ 代入。推广后的复杂度为 $O(1+Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$。

**内存需求。** 在附录 C 中，我们推导了能够保证所有层 FPR 均小于 1 的内存需求 $M/N$。与上一小节相同，推广后的结果是每个条目 1.62 比特，远低于主流系统中的默认比率。在附录 C 中，我们还说明了如何使流体 LSM 树适应内存极少的环境。

**范围查询。** 一次短范围查询在较小的 $L-1$ 层中，每层至多发出 $K$ 次 I/O，在最大层至多发出 $Z$ 次 I/O，因而总计产生 $Z+K\cdot(L-1)$ 次随机 I/O，成本复杂度为 $O(Z+K\cdot(L-1))$。长范围查询还会继续在每个有序游程中对相关键范围进行顺序扫描，至少发出 $s/B$ 次顺序 I/O，其中 $s$ 是目标键范围内唯一条目的数量。为了计入过时条目，第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层中已更新条目造成的顺序 I/O 数量会放大 $1+1/T$ 倍，第 $L$ 层中则会放大 $Z$ 倍；我们将二者合并建模为 $Z+1/T$。因此，顺序扫描成本至多为 $s/B\cdot(Z+1/T)$ 次 I/O，复杂度为 $O(s\cdot Z/B)$ 次 I/O。推广后的范围查询成本由公式 11 给出，它是短范围查询与长范围查询成本之和，并使用常数 $\mu$ 加权；$\mu$ 表示在给定存储设备（例如磁盘）上顺序访问相对于随机访问快多少。

$$
Q=K\cdot(L-1)+Z+\frac{1}{\mu}\cdot\frac{s}{B}\cdot\left(Z+\frac{1}{T}\right).
\tag{11}
$$

**更新。** 在最坏情况下，一个条目在第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的每一层中，都会在活跃有序游程内参与 $O(T/K)$ 次合并操作；在第 $L$ 层的活跃有序游程内则参与 $O(T/Z)$ 次合并操作。总更新成本是这些项跨所有层的总和再除以块大小，即 $O\left(\frac{T}{B}\cdot\left(\frac{L}{K}+\frac{1}{Z}\right)\right)$。我们使用等差数列更精确地对该成本建模，得到公式 12，并在附录 I 中进行验证。由于更新成本是通过顺序合并操作产生的，我们除以常数 $\mu$；此外还引入常数 $\phi$，以反映某些存储设备（例如闪存）上写入比读取更昂贵这一特性。

$$
W=\frac{\phi}{\mu\cdot B}\cdot\left(\frac{T-1}{K+1}\cdot(L-1)+\frac{T-1}{Z+1}\right).
\tag{12}
$$

**空间放大。** 第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层包含数据集的 $1/T$，因此它们最多可能使最大层中同等比例的条目变为过时条目。在第 $L$ 层中，至多有 $Z-1$ 个有序游程可能完全由过时条目填满。我们在公式 13 中将这些项相加，对空间放大进行建模。

$$
\operatorname{amp}=Z-1+\frac{1}{T}.
\tag{13}
$$

**映射设计空间。** 图 9 是概念图 7 的一个具体实例，它使用我们的成本模型来映射流体 LSM 树中的不同权衡。图 9(A) 通过绘制公式 10 中的点查询成本 $R$ 与公式 12 中的更新成本 $W$ 得到。图 9(B) 和图 9(C) 分别针对选择率 $s$ 为 $10^{-7}$ 和 $10^{-6}$ 的短范围查询与长范围查询，绘制公式 11 中的 $Q$ 与公式 12 中的更新成本 $W$。空间放大的评估留待实验分析。配置固定为 1 TB 数据集、128 字节条目、4 KB 存储块，并且所有过滤器合计为每个条目 10 比特。⁵ 我们为参数 $K$ 和 $Z$ 使用分级式（leveling）、分层式（tiering）和惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）各自对应的固定值，并改变大小比率 $T$，从而生成这些曲线。圆点表示三种合并策略在大小比率 $T$ 设为 2 时的汇合点。方块表示大小比率为 10；实践中大多数主流键值存储默认采用该值 [29, 32]，由此可以比较三个子图中的对应点。图中还展示了两条标为 Trans1 和 Trans2 的过渡曲线，它们说明流体 LSM 树如何在不同设计之间平滑过渡，从而实现使用固定合并策略时无法达到的权衡。

**图 9：在惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）、分级式（leveling）与分层式（tiering）之间平滑切换。**

**过渡 1：从惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）到分层式（tiering）。** 我们在图 9(A) 中观察到，惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）的曲线存在一个拐点；越过该点后，减小大小比率反而会恶化更新成本。原因是更新成本为 $O((L+T)/B)$，当我们减小 $T$ 时，$L$ 的值会增长并逐渐主导 $T$。在该示例中，拐点出现在大小比率 $T$ 设为 5 时。我们将 $T$ 固定为拐点值，转而让 $Z$ 从 1 变化到 $T-1$（本例中为 4），由此生成标为过渡 1（Trans1）的曲线。在这部分设计空间中，所得曲线同时优于惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）和分层式（tiering），直至与分层式（tiering）汇合。因此，当我们在惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）和分层式（tiering）之间过渡、分别进一步针对点查询或更新进行优化时，过渡 1 能够实现点查询成本与更新成本之间的最优权衡。

**过渡 2：从惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）到分级式（leveling）。** 为了使惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）获得更具竞争力的范围查询成本，我们引入过渡 2。其思想是让 $K$（第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的有序游程数上界）在 1 与 $T-1$ 之间变化，从而在惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）与分级式（leveling）之间平滑过渡。在图 9 中，我们将 $K$ 固定为 4 并改变 $T$，生成标为 Trans2 的曲线。图 9(A) 表明，这使系统能够沿着一条与惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）相似的权衡曲线移动；图 9(B) 和图 9(C) 则表明，Trans2 获得了与分级式（leveling）几乎相同的范围查询成本。因此，过渡 2 能够精细控制对短范围查询的优化程度。

**问题：寻找最佳调优配置。** 最优时空权衡的空间由分级式（leveling）、惰性分级（Lazy Leveling）和分层式（tiering）划定；通过联合调节参数 $K$、$Z$ 和 $T$，我们还能到达其间的许多其他权衡点。目标是联合调节这些参数，为特定应用取得最佳权衡。挑战在于这些参数存在许多不同组合，因而难以找到最优配置。⁶

⁵ 我们设置 $N=2^{33}$、$E=2^7$、$B=2^5$，且 $M=10\cdot2^{33}$。

⁶ 组合数量为 $O((N/(B\cdot P))^3)$。原因是 $T$ 的最大值为 $T_{lim}=N/(B\cdot P)$，而 $K$ 和 $Z$ 都可以调节为 1 到 $T-1$ 之间的任意值。

sections.title: 4.3 Dostoevsky
sections.originalMarkdown:
We now introduce Dostoevsky to find and adapt to the best tuning of Fluid LSM-tree subject to a constraint on space-amplification. Dostoevsky models and optimizes throughput with respect to update cost $W$ in Equation 12, zero-result point lookup cost $R$ in Equation 10, non-zero result point lookup cost $V$ in Equation 8, and range lookup cost $Q$ in Equation 11. It monitors the proportion of these operations in the workload and weights their costs using coefficients $w$, $r$, $v$, and $q$, respectively. We multiply this weighted cost by the time to read a block from storage $\Omega$ and taking the inverse to obtain the weighted worst-case throughput $\tau$.

$$
\tau=\Omega^{-1}\cdot(w\cdot W+r\cdot R+v\cdot V+q\cdot Q)^{-1}.
\tag{14}
$$

Dostoevsky maximizes Equation 14 by iterating over different values of the parameters $T$, $K$, and $Z$. It prunes the search space using two insights.
